perturbation.original_text: The term “ Representative ” means, as to any person, such person’s affiliates, officers, directors, general partners, members, employees, consultants, investment bankers, financial advisors, accountants, legal counsel, consultants and potential lenders.
perturbation.changed_text: The term “ Representative ” means, as to any person, such person's affiliates, officers, directors, general partners, members, and employees. Consultants, investment bankers, financial advisors, accountants, legal counsel, and potential lenders are explicitly excluded.
perturbation.explanation: By explicitly excluding 'consultants, investment bankers, financial advisors, accountants, legal counsel, and potential lenders' from the definition of 'Representative,' the contract attempts to narrow the scope of who is bound by the confidentiality obligations. This can lead to potential breaches if these excluded parties mishandle proprietary information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 12b-2 defines 'affiliate' broadly, encompassing anyone who directly or indirectly controls, is controlled by, or is under common control with the specified person. By excluding certain key roles, the modified text contradicts the intent of securities regulations to ensure comprehensive coverage in matters of information and control.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.12b-2 Definitions. Unless the context otherwise requires, the following terms, when used in the rules contained in this regulation or in Regulation 13A or 15D or in the forms for statements and reports filed pursuant to sections 12, 13 or 15(d) of the act, shall have the respective meanings indicated in this rule: Accelerated filer and large accelerated filer —(1) Accelerated filer. The term accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $75 million or more, but less than $700 million, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78m or 78o(d) ) for a period of at least twelve calendar months; and (iii) The issuer has filed at least one annual report pursuant to section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act; and (iv) The issuer is not eligible to use the requirements for smaller reporting companies under the revenue test in paragraph (2) or (3)(iii)(B) of the “smaller reporting company” definition in this section , as applicable. (2) Large accelerated filer. The term large accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $700 million or more, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) o
perturbation.justification: The original contract text defines 'Representative' broadly, including consultants, investment bankers, financial advisors, accountants, legal counsel, and potential lenders. The changed text explicitly excludes these roles. The Law Explanation states that Rule 12b-2 defines 'affiliate' broadly to ensure comprehensive coverage. By excluding key roles from the definition of 'Representative,' the modified text contradicts the intent of securities regulations, as it narrows the scope of who is bound by confidentiality obligations and potentially undermines the comprehensive coverage intended by regulations like Rule 12b-2. The scraped law snippet 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions defines terms when used in rules contained in the regulation, showing the broad nature of the law being applied.

perturbation.original_text: Proprietary Information does not include, however, information that (i) was or becomes available to the Receiving Party on a non-confidential basis from a source other than the Disclosing Party or any of its Representatives, which source the Receiving Party does not know, after reasonable inquiry, to be in violation of any legal duty or obligation owed to the Disclosing Party or any of its Representatives with respect to such information
perturbation.changed_text: Proprietary Information does not include, however, information that (i) was or becomes available to the Receiving Party on a non-confidential basis from any source, regardless of any potential violation of legal duty or obligation owed to the Disclosing Party or any of its Representatives with respect to such information
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires a 'reasonable inquiry' to ensure the source isn't violating any duty to the Disclosing Party. Removing this requirement broadens the exception, allowing the Receiving Party to use information even if the source is acting unlawfully. This weakens the protection of proprietary information.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change weakens the protection of trade secrets. The Economic Espionage Act defines trade secrets and provides penalties for their theft or misappropriation. By allowing the Receiving Party to use information obtained from any source, regardless of legal duty, the modified text could potentially conflict with the Act's provisions against the unlawful acquisition of trade secrets.
perturbation.justification: The change in the contract text removes the requirement for the Receiving Party to conduct a 'reasonable inquiry' to ensure the source of the information is not violating any legal duty to the Disclosing Party. The Law Explanation states this weakens the protection of trade secrets and could conflict with provisions against the unlawful acquisition of trade secrets, as potentially covered under the Economic Espionage Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references the Economic Espionage Act of 1996 (18 U.S.C. 1 note). By removing the 'reasonable inquiry' clause, the modified contract potentially allows for the use of illegally obtained information, directly contradicting the intent of laws protecting trade secrets. Therefore, a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Upon either party’s request, each party hereto shall (and shall cause its Representatives, and any other person, to whom it has disclosed any Proprietary Information or Transaction Information) promptly either (at the Receiving Party’s option) return to the other party hereto or destroy (and certify in writing to the other party by an authorized officer supervising such destruction) all copies or other reproductions of Proprietary 2 Information of the other party, other than any Derivative Materials, in its possession or the possession of any of its Representatives, and shall not retain any copies or other reproductions, in whole or in part, of such materials.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon either party’s request, each party hereto shall (and shall cause its Representatives, and any other person, to whom it has disclosed any Proprietary Information or Transaction Information) make a good faith effort to return to the other party hereto or destroy all copies or other reproductions of Proprietary Information of the other party in its possession. Certification of destruction is optional.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing 'promptly return or destroy' with 'make a good faith effort' and making certification optional, the contract introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to protect proprietary information. The original text provides a clear and enforceable requirement, while the modification introduces subjectivity.
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 34 deals with the production of documents and tangible things. The modified text's vague language about 'good faith effort' and 'optional certification' could conflict with discovery obligations under FRCP 34, which requires a reasonable and diligent search for and production of relevant documents. The relaxed standard could be interpreted as a failure to comply with discovery requirements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 34. Producing Documents, Electronically Stored Information, and Tangible Things, or Entering onto Land, for Inspection and Other Purposes | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 34. Producing Documents, Electronically Stored Information, and Tangible Things, or Entering onto Land, for Inspection and Other Purposes (a) In General. A party may serve on any other party a request within the scope of Rule 26(b) : (1) to produce and permit the requesting party or its representative to inspect, copy, test, or sample the following items in the responding party's possession, custody, or control: (A) any designated documents or electronically stored information—including writings, drawings, graphs, charts, photographs, sound recordings, images, and other data or data compilations—stored in any medium from which information can be obtained either directly or, if necessary, after translation by the responding party into a reasonably usable form; or (B) any designated tangible things; or (2) to permit entry onto designated land or other property possessed or controlled by the responding party, so that the requesting party may inspect, measure, survey, photograph, test, or sample the property or any designated object or operation on it. (b) Procedure. (1) Contents of the Request. The request: (A) must describe with reasonable particularity each item or category of items to be inspected; (B) must specify a reasonable time, place, and manner for the inspection and for performing the related acts; and (C) may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (2) Responses and Objections. (A) Time to Respond. The party to whom the request is directed must respond in writing within 30 days after being served or — if the request was delivered under Rule 26(d)(2) — within 30 days after the parties’ first Rule 26(f) conference. A shorter or longer time may be s

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The original text mandates a prompt return or destruction of proprietary information, with a written certification of destruction. The changed text weakens this obligation by requiring only a 'good faith effort' to return or destroy the information, and makes certification optional. This introduces ambiguity and reduces the enforceability of the provision. The Law Explanation points out that Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 34 requires a reasonable and diligent search for and production of relevant documents. The modified text's vague language conflicts with these discovery obligations, as a 'good faith effort' may not be sufficient to meet the requirements of a 'reasonable and diligent search.' Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing Rule 34, details the requirements for producing documents, including 'any designated documents or electronically stored information.' The changed text creates a contradiction because it potentially allows a party to avoid fully complying with Rule 34's production requirements due to the weakened obligation to return or destroy proprietary information.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be governed by the laws of the State of New York and will be binding upon and inure to the benefit of the parties hereto and their respective successors and assigns.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be governed by the laws of the State of Delaware, irrespective of conflicts of law principles, and will be binding upon and inure to the benefit of only Fairfax Financial Holdings Limited.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware while ignoring conflicts of law principles and limiting the benefit to only one party alters the enforceability and fairness of the agreement. The original provides for mutual benefit and predictable interpretation, while the modification is one-sided and potentially unpredictable.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change could impact venue and jurisdiction. 28 U.S.C. § 1391 governs venue in federal courts, taking into account the location of defendants, where a substantial part of the events occurred, or where the property is located. While parties can agree to a forum selection clause, specifying Delaware law 'irrespective of conflicts of law principles' might conflict with the court's analysis under § 1391 if the events or property related to the dispute are overwhelmingly located elsewhere. Also by stating it only benefits Fairfax, the other party's right to sue or enforce the contract could be challenged.

Venue generally (a) Applicability of Section .-Except as otherwise provided by law- (1) this section shall govern the venue of all civil actions brought in district courts of the United States; and (2) the proper venue for a civil action shall be determined without regard to whether the action is local or transitory in nature. (b) Venue in General .-A civil action may be brought in- (1) a judicial district in which a
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the governing law and limits the agreement's benefit to only one party, potentially conflicting with venue and jurisdiction principles. 28 U.S.C. § 1391 addresses venue in federal courts, considering the location of defendants and events. The LLM explanation suggests that specifying Delaware law while disregarding conflict of law principles could clash with § 1391 if the dispute's events or property are primarily located elsewhere. Moreover, restricting the benefit to Fairfax Financial Holdings Limited might impede the other party's ability to sue or enforce the contract, as per the explanation. The scraped snippet of 28 USC 1391 confirms that venue is generally governed by this section in civil actions, and the changed contract language attempts to circumvent that by specifying Delaware law irrespective of conflicts of law principles. Therefore, the modification creates a contradiction.
